original_hypothesis.title: Reentrancy via validateCredential Provider Callback Can Corrupt Lender Status Storage
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: _tryValidateCredential makes an external CALL (not staticcall) to a role provider's validateCredential function, passing all remaining gas. Since the lender's status is read into memory before this call and written to storage after it returns, a malicious provider can reenter the hooks contract during the callback to modify _lenderStatus in storage. When the original function resumes, _writeLenderStatus overwrites the storage with stale in-memory data, reverting any changes made during reentrancy. This enables a provider to corrupt credential state by having grantRole called during the callback and then having it overwritten.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The call pattern is: read _lenderStatus to memory → external CALL to provider → modify memory status → write memory status to storage. This classic read-modify-write pattern with an external call in the middle is vulnerable to reentrancy if the external call can modify the same storage slot.

proof_of_signal.pattern: External CALL to provider between reading _lenderStatus to memory and writing it back to storage. Provider is a registered role provider that can call grantRole during callback.

preconditions: A registered role provider is a contract that reenters the hooks contract during validateCredential
The provider calls grantRole(victim, timestamp) during the callback
The original function's _writeLenderStatus overwrites the grantRole's storage write

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: External call between storage read and storage write; no reentrancy guard
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Provider can call grantRole during validateCredential callback, but the in-memory status from before the callback overwrites the grant when _writeLenderStatus executes
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No reentrancy guard (nonReentrant modifier) on hooks functions

analysis_questions: Can a provider realistically reenter grantRole during the validateCredential callback?
Does the overwrite actually downgrade or corrupt the lender's credential?
Are there other functions the provider could reenter to cause harm?
validation_requirements: Deploy a malicious provider that calls grantRole during validateCredential
Verify that _writeLenderStatus overwrites the grantRole storage update
Check if the market contract has reentrancy guards that prevent this path

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Malicious provider is added by borrower. During onDeposit, lender provides hooksData pointing to malicious provider. Provider's validateCredential calls grantRole(lender, farFutureTimestamp) to give a long-lived credential, then returns a short-lived timestamp. The hooks contract first processes grantRole (writing long credential to storage), then _tryValidateCredential gets the short timestamp, and _writeLenderStatus overwrites with the short credential, silently downgrading the lender.
prerequisites: Borrower adds a malicious/compromised role provider
Lender uses that provider for credential validation

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Credential state corruption could lock lenders out of deposits or withdrawals
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Lender must re-authenticate with a different provider

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No reentrancy tests for provider callbacks

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Gas forwarded to provider includes enough for reentrant grantRole call
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Multiple events emitted in single transaction would indicate reentrancy

validation_result.mechanism: reentrancy via provider callback overwrites storage
validation_result.impact_summary: A registered role provider can reenter grantRole/revokeRole during _tryValidateCredential callback, but those storage writes to _lenderStatus are silently overwritten when _writeLenderStatus executes afterwards with stale memory data.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The vulnerability follows a classic read-modify-write reentrancy pattern. In both AccessControlHooks.sol and FixedTermLoanHooks.sol, when credential validation occurs via onDeposit/onTransfer/onQueueWithdrawal, the lender's status is read from storage into memory at the start of the hook function. The _tryValidateCredential function then makes an external CALL (not staticcall) to the provider's validateCredential function, forwarding all remaining gas. During this callback, the provider (a registered role provider contract) can reenter the hooks contract to call grantRole, revokeRole, or even blockFromDeposits (if the provider is also the borrower). These reentrant calls read fresh _lenderStatus from storage, modify it, and write it back. However, when the external call returns, the original function resumes with its stale memory copy, modifies it based on the validateCredential return value, and _writeLenderStatus overwrites storage with the stale data. This silently discards any storage changes made during reentrancy.

The code lacks any reentrancy guard (nonReentrant modifier or equivalent). The defect exists regardless of whether a profitable exploit can be constructed — the code is wrong because it permits storage writes to be silently overwritten.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof-of-signal is correct. Both AccessControlHooks.sol and FixedTermLoanHooks.sol contain the vulnerable pattern: (1) storage read to memory, (2) external CALL to provider, (3) memory write back to storage. The assembly block in _tryValidateCredential uses `call(gas(), providerAddress, ...)` which forwards all gas and allows reentrancy. No reentrancy guard exists on any of the affected functions.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The lender status is read into memory before the external call to the provider in _tryValidateCredential. Any storage modifications to _lenderStatus[lender] during the callback (via grantRole/revokeRole) are overwritten when _writeLenderStatus executes with the stale memory data.

External CALL to providerAddress forwards all remaining gas, enabling the provider to execute arbitrary logic including reentrant calls to grantRole or revokeRole on the hooks contract. The call is not a staticcall, so the provider can modify state.
This line overwrites storage with the memory-based status object that was read BEFORE the external call. Any storage changes made during the provider callback are silently lost.

No reentrancy guard. A registered provider can call this during the _tryValidateCredential callback. The storage write performed by _setCredentialAndEmitAccessGranted will be overwritten when the outer function's _writeLenderStatus executes.

calling_sequence: 5. _tryValidateCredential makes external CALL to providerAddress.validateCredential(lender, data) with all gas
7. grantRole reads FRESH _lenderStatus[lender] from storage, calls _grantRole
8. _setCredentialAndEmitAccessGranted writes updated status to storage: _lenderStatus[lender] = status_new
9. Control returns to _tryValidateCredential, provider returned a short timestamp
10. status.setCredential(provider, shortTimestamp) modifies ORIGINAL memory status (from step 1)
11. Back in onDeposit, _writeLenderStatus is called with the stale memory status
12. _lenderStatus[lender] = status (stale memory) OVERWRITES the grantRole storage write from step 8
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path is: onDeposit → _tryValidateAccessInner → _handleHooksData → _tryValidateCredential → external CALL to provider → [provider reenters grantRole → _grantRole → _setCredentialAndEmitAccessGranted → WRITE to storage] → back in _tryValidateCredential → status.setCredential in memory → back in onDeposit → _writeLenderStatus → OVERWRITE storage with stale memory. The storage write from grantRole in step 8 is silently lost.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that are relevant to this vulnerability. The defect is in the contract code itself.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A malicious role provider added by the borrower can exploit this vulnerability. Scenario: (1) Provider is registered via addRoleProvider. (2) Lender's credential has expired. (3) Lender calls deposit with hooksData pointing to the malicious provider. (4) During the validateCredential callback, the provider calls grantRole(lender, farFutureTimestamp) which writes a long-lived credential to storage. (5) Provider returns a short-lived timestamp from validateCredential. (6) _tryValidateCredential updates the in-memory status with the short credential. (7) _writeLenderStatus overwrites storage with the short credential, silently discarding the long-lived one. The provider could also call revokeRole during the callback (if it was the lastProvider), and that revocation would similarly be overwritten. The net effect is that any storage modification to _lenderStatus during the provider callback is silently lost.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The impact is credential state corruption for lenders. A malicious provider can cause: (1) grantRole writes to be silently overwritten, meaning credential upgrades are lost; (2) revokeRole writes to be silently overwritten, meaning a revoked credential could be restored with different parameters; (3) potential inconsistency between events emitted during reentrancy and actual storage state. This could lead to lenders having shorter credential durations than expected, or events that don't match actual state. The severity is HIGH because it violates INV-ACCESS-002 (credential replacement rules) — a provider can effectively downgrade a credential by exploiting the stale-memory overwrite.
recommended_mitigations: Add a reentrancy guard (nonReentrant modifier) to all state-modifying external functions including grantRole, grantRoles, revokeRole, blockFromDeposits, unblockFromDeposits, and all hook entry points (onDeposit, onTransfer, onQueueWithdrawal).

Alternative: Perform the storage write (_writeLenderStatus) BEFORE the external call to the provider, following the checks-effects-interactions pattern. Then re-read from storage after the call if needed.

Alternative: Use staticcall instead of call for _tryValidateCredential if the provider's validateCredential does not genuinely need to be stateful (though the current comment explicitly says it IS stateful).
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy AccessControlHooks with a borrower address. 2. Create a MaliciousProvider contract whose validateCredential() reenters hooks.grantRole(lender, type(uint32).max) and then returns a timestamp of 1 (minimal credential). 3. Register MaliciousProvider via addRoleProvider. 4. Deploy a market and register it. 5. Call onDeposit from the market with hooksData pointing to MaliciousProvider. 6. After the call, read _lenderStatus[lender] and verify that the grantRole's far-future credential was overwritten with the short-lived credential. 7. Verify that two AccountAccessGranted events were emitted (one from grantRole, one from _writeLenderStatus) but storage reflects only the short credential.
uncertainties: The practical impact depends on whether borrowers would add malicious providers. However, the code defect exists regardless.
The Market contract (msg.sender for hook calls) may have its own reentrancy guards that prevent certain attack paths, but the hooks contract itself is unprotected.
The same provider being both the caller of grantRole and the provider being validated creates a somewhat self-defeating attack scenario, but the code pattern is still wrong.
additional_testing_needed: Test reentrancy from provider during validateCredential callback calling grantRole for the same lender
Test reentrancy calling revokeRole during callback to verify the revocation is also overwritten
Test cross-function reentrancy: provider calls blockFromDeposits during callback (if provider is also borrower)
Verify gas forwarding is sufficient for the reentrant grantRole call to complete successfully
